ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean fried tau kwa (firm beancurd). FOODON_03302025 (fried tofu food product) is the closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried soybean curd broadly; used as closest defensible code for deep-fried firm tofu (tau kwa).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried tau kwa; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried tau kwa is one piece weighing approximately 80 g, commonly served as a side dish, snack, or component in dishes such as yong tau foo or rojak.

nutrient_source.title: Fried tau kwa — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Fried tau kwa' (firm beancurd, deep-fried). Confidence 0.95 as reported in input record.

gi_source.title: Tofu, plain — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for fried tau kwa (deep-fried firm tofu). GI of approximately 15 is estimated from plain tofu entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI in the range of 15 for soy-based tofu products. Deep-frying does not materially alter the carbohydrate matrix of firm tofu. Carbohydrate content is 0.66 g/100g; glycemic load per 80 g serving = (15 × 0.66 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 0.08, which is negligible.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 278 mg/100g is borderline for hypertensive individuals; limit portion to one piece (≈80 g) and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments such as dark soy sauce or sambal to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2,000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.66 g/100g) and zero sugar with high plant protein (16.76 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried tau kwa has a negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 0.08 per 80 g serving) and high plant protein that supports satiety and blood glucose stability, making it a suitable protein choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; avoid sweet dipping sauces that add sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds fat and potential oxidised lipids; soy isoflavones are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While soy protein and isoflavones in tau kwa support LDL reduction, deep-frying increases total fat and may introduce oxidised lipids; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and prefer steamed or pan-seared preparations where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation disqualifies this food for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE formation; choose steamed, braised, or pan-seared firm tofu to retain the anti-inflammatory benefits of soy isoflavones and calcium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried tau kwa is a popular Singaporean and Chinese dish consisting of firm beancurd (tau kwa) that has been deep-fried until the exterior is golden-brown and slightly crispy while the interior remains dense and chewy. Tau kwa is a pressed, extra-firm variety of tofu made from coagulated soy milk, with a higher protein density than silken or soft tofu. Deep-frying creates a textured crust that absorbs sauces and seasonings well, making it a versatile ingredient in dishes such as yong tau foo, rojak, and tau kwa pau. Nutritionally, fried tau kwa is high in plant protein (16.76 g/100g), low in carbohydrates (0.66 g/100g), and contains zero sugar, making it an excellent protein source for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals managing blood glucose. Its glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible. Sodium at 278 mg/100g is borderline and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The deep-frying process adds fat and may generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease. Soy isoflavones present in tau kwa have documented cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory properties when the food is not deep-fried. Fried tau kwa is commonly served as a snack, side dish, or protein component in hawker-centre meals across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat fried tau kwa?
How many calories in fried tau kwa?

Is tau kwa suitable for vegetarians?
What is tau kwa made of?

Can I eat fried tau kwa if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between tau kwa and tau pok?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95 as reported in the canonical input record. Exact match to 'Fried tau kwa' entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain tofu, GI ≈ 15) due to absence of a direct fried tau kwa GI entry; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — firm tofu contains no free sugars by composition. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food product with no animal-derived ingredients. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), individual B-vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), vitamin C, and mineral micronutrients (phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), as well as moisture content, are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for fried tau kwa as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172475, tofu firm prepared with calcium sulfate) was also consulted and does not provide complete fatty acid fractions or the full micronutrient panel for the deep-fried preparation. These fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data unavailability rather than a confirmed zero value. potassium_mg (150 mg), calcium_mg (200 mg), and iron_mg (2.0 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tofu, firm, prepared with calcium sulfate (FDC ID 172475) as a supporting reference, consistent with the HPB record. saturated_fat_g (1.3 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for firm tofu, deep-fried, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain tofu entries); no direct GI measurement for fried tau kwa is available in any approved source. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (15 × 0.66 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 0.08.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Fried Tau Kwa' is precise and unambiguous. Mandatory Singaporean-dish local-language names present: Chinese simplified (炸豆干), Chinese traditional (炸豆乾), Malay (Tauhu goreng keras), Tamil (வறுத்த தோஃபு), Hindi (तला हुआ टोफू).

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (147.0), protein_g (16.76), fat_g (8.63), carbohydrate_g (0.66) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (exact match, confidence 0.95).

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.7), sodium_mg (278.0), saturated_fat_g (1.3), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. sugar_g confirmed zero per HPB; saturated_fat_g estimated from USDA SR Legacy as documented in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (15), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (0.08), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (plain tofu, GI ≈ 15); estimation basis fully documented. Carbohydrate content (0.66 g/100g) is non-negligible and a GI estimate is defensible.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03302025 (fried tofu food product) used as closest_parent match with gap note. FoodEx2 A00LC (soybean curd, fried) used as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium-focused), Type 2 Diabetes (low, negligible GL and high plant protein), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, deep-frying fat and isoflavone balance). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly under machine key type2_diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 13 entries covering English variants, Chinese script forms, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for all mandatory Singaporean-dish languages. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — 'Fried tau kwa', 'Fried firm tofu', 'Deep-fried firm beancurd', 'Deep-fried tau kwa', 'Fried firm bean curd', 'Crispy fried tofu', '炸豆干', '炸豆乾', 'Tauhu goreng keras', 'Tau kwa goreng', 'வறுத்த தோஃபு', 'तला हुआ टोफू', 'Zha dou gan' — all confirmed present in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built initial RESULT JSON from input, populated all mandatory sections, added multilingual names, health context, ontology, and LLM training fields.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added -1 documentation to notes field to address g3_numeric_hygiene failures.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document every -1 field with (a) the specific field name, (b) the reason data is unavailable, and (c) the approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability (HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172475), fully satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene requirements per the negative sentinel values rule.
